content:
Image Source: ChatGPT
A lightweight agentic workflow for transforming blog articles into AI-generated podcasts using Agno, Gemini, Firecrawl, and ElevenLabs.

6 min read
Just now

--

Reading tech blogs is one of my absolute favorite ways to learn. Whenever I see a deep dive into a new framework or someone sharing a neat AI project, I immediately bookmark it.
But let’s be honest,
I rarely get the quiet time to sit down and read them without interruptions. Listening just fits my routine much better. I can consume those articles while I’m commuting, working out, or out for a walk.
That got me thinking… 💭
What if a blog could become a podcast with a single click❓
Rather than manually copying content between different tools, summarizing it, and then converting it into audio as 3 separate tasks, I always wanted to build a lightweight AI application that could automate the entire process.
That’s way, I’ve combined them into a single agentic workflow. The user simply provides a blog URL, and the agent takes care of the rest behind the scenes.

From 🔗 URL ➔ Podcast 🎙️
At a high level, the app does just four things:

• Extract the blog content.

• Turns it into a podcast-friendly summary.

• Convert that summary into natural speech.

• And it returns you an MP3 you can play or download.
The overall pipeline is surprisingly straightforward, each stage is handled by a tool that is already great at those jobs and focused on connecting them into one smooth, end-to-end workflow.
Diagram 1 — Overall Architecture
Blog URL
|
▼
Firecrawl
|
▼
nGemini 2.5 Flash
|
▼
Podcast Script
|
▼
ElevenLabs
|
▼
MP3 PodcastThis separation of responsibilities keeps the application modular. If one component changes. e.g., replacing the language model or supporting a different speech provider , the rest of the pipeline remains largely unaffected.
Choosing the Right Building Blocks
The project combies a few focused tools, each responsible for a specific part of the workflow.

• Streamlit provides a simple interface where users can enter their API key,s paste a blog URL, and listen to the generated podcast.

• Firecrawl extracts the main content from the blog, avoiding the complexity of parsing raw HTML.

• Gemini 2.5 Flash understands the article and rewrites it into a concise, podcast-friendly script.

• ElevenLabs converts the generated script into natural speech.

• Agno orchestrates the entire workflow, allowing the application to focus on a single objective rather than coordinating individual API calls.
Each tool does one job well, and together they form a simple yet effective pipeline for transforming written content into an audio experience.
Why Use an Agent?
Looking at the workflow, you might wonder whether an agent is even necessary. After all, the application could be simply call three API’s in sequence: scrape the blog, summarize it, and generate the audio.
That approach would work.
However, using an agent lets the app focus on the goal rather than the individual steps.
Instead of manually orchestrating each API call, the agent receives a simple objective: turn this blog into a podcast. It uses the available tools to extract the article, generate a podcast-friendly script, and pass it on for speech synthesis.

Diagram 2 — Agent Workflow
User Request
|
▼
Agno Agent
|
▼
Extract Blog
|
▼
Generate Podcast Script
|
▼
Create Audio
|
▼
Return MP3Overall, with the architecture in place, the next step is understanding how these individual components come together to transform simple blog URL content into a podcast-ready audio experience.
Designing the Workflow
With the architecture in place, the implementation is surprisingly simple, The application begins by validating the required API keys and accepting a blog URL from the user.
Once everything is ready, it creates an Agno agent equipped with Gemini and Firecrawl.
agent = Agent(
name=”Blog Summarizer”,
model=Gemini(id=”gemini-2.5-flash”),
tools=[FirecrawlTools()],
)After, given an blog URL, It extracts the article, generates a podcast-friendly summary, and passes the final script to ElevenLabs for speech synthesis.
From📄Article ➔ Podcast Script📜
One choice I spent a ton of time on wasn’t about the LLM itself, but the final output. I had to decide:
should the app just summarize the text, or build something that sounds like an actual podcast?
It turns out those are two completely different jobs.
A classic summary is built for your eyes, but a podcast script is built for your ears. Big paragraphs and formal text just sound weird out loud.
Instead of asking Gemini for a summary, the prompt guides it to write a short, conversational script. That shift makes a massive difference. It stops the audio from sounding like a robotic reading and makes it feel like a real show.

Diagram 3 — Content Transformation
Blog URL
|
▼
Clean Article
|
▼
Podcast Script
|
▼
Natural Speech
|
▼
MP3Each step builds on the previous one. Gradually converting structured text into podcast-ready audio.
Generating the Audio🎵
After the script is generated, it goes straight to ElevenLabs to be converted into realistic speech.
Then, the audio streams right back to the app, letting you play it immediately or download it as an MP3. Just keep in mind that the final quality isn’t just about the AI voice. the script matters just as much.
A well-structured, conversational script is what makes the whole thing feel like a real show.
Putting Everything Together
One thing I really like about this project is how minimal the code it uses. It doesn’t rely on complex machine learning algorithms or complex scraping logic to get the job done.
Instead, it focuses on orchestrating specialized AI services into a single experience.
From the user’s perspective, the workflow looks remarkably simple:

• Paste a blog URL.

• Click Generate Podcast.

• Listen to or download the result.
Behind those 3 steps, The app coordinates,

• web scraping

• language understanding

• prompt engineering, and speech synthesis
before returning the final podcast.

Diagram 4 — Sequence Diagram
Image Source: AuthorLooking at the sequence diagram, the project isn’t about any individual model or service. It’s about how these components work together to deliver a seamless user experience.
Current Limitations
It currently supports only blog URLs and generates a single-speaker podcast.
The features like,

• PDF support

• Multilingual narration

• Multiple AI voices, or customizable podcast lengths
Could make it even more versatile.
Where This Can Go Next
Honestly, the coolest thing about using an agent is how easy it is to add new stuff without messing up the entire workflow.
Here are a few ideas that would plug right in:

• PDF and research paper support

• RSS feed ingestion

• Multilingual podcast generation

• Multiple AI hosts discussing an article

• Voice selection and cloning

• Background music

• Automatic episode titles

• Publishing directly to podcast platforms
Since we kept the setup modular from the start, we can just stack these features on top without having to rebuild the whole pipeline.
